author: @ZsOtherBrother
text:
Wow, this means we can make solar panels that are also Peltier devices, and turn the problem of the panel heating up into an advantage. If there are non toxic materials that have these properties, they could be spray painted onto a roof or the side of buildings to turn them into energy collectors... And if we add to that @Martin P. Hellwig's comment (from about two years ago), we get a "smart panel" that can include its own optimizing circuits.
I find this very exciting.

author: @NeoShameMan
text:
That's quite amazing and inspiring! so we can make transistor with that too? Turns out all the piece are falling into place lol!
One thing with energy production is we either produce more of them to cover our need, our consume less of them. A lot of idea seems to be turned toward maximum production efficiency, and a lot of accessible technique get discarded because they aren't efficient enough, which make economic sense at industrial scale. BUT I feel like there is an opportunity at human scale to look into the direction of "slow tech".

While I was watching my plant growing in my experiment, it didn't really need a lot input, it really tooks it's time, but the plant at enough force to remove the lead on top of the pot, what if we create autonomous machine that just took their time like plants, they charge slow, but have enough energy to do work that aren't in an hurry. For example imagine an autonomous garden that slowly charge over time, taking energy directly from soil battery (just an example) then discharge it in time of harvest lol.
There is certainly a lot of essential infrastructure that can be turn autonomous with just slow system?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I would think so mate - certainly something to try

author: @scotttovey
text:
I'm thinking that you could nearly double the voltage output by taking a rectangular coper solar cell and painting half of it with the yellow lead paste. 
Hmm?  Would that constitute series or parallel?
Maybe that would increase your amp output instead?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: both I would think

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering 
That's a needs to be tested to find out situation. 
It may depend on where you put the jumper lead on the on the single side. 
At the far end a series setup would occur.
At the half way point where the yellow lead adheres to the coper, a parallel setup would occur.
Putting three leads on it, one at either end and one in the middle, a combination of series and parallel setup would occur.

author: @kreynolds1123
text: One nice thing about the NP junction solar cells is that solar thermal electrons  can't return  because of the NP junction recitificication. Electrons can only return through an external circuit.

I remember when i was in perhaps 4th or 5th primary i build a small cell when i bruised the +ive and _ive wires spark and it survived 10-20 minutes also  turned hot unable to hold in hand. 
At that time i searched lot to explore these things due to the unavailability of internet, i read books and learnt internal resistance, electrode potential etc. I guess at that time something was making my cell to leak the charge through internal resistance hence (I square R loss). 
I left my experiment just by doubting the imperfections with my salt bridge. 
Sir u recall me all the work i was doing 20,23 years ago. I like it 👍

author: @dav1dbone
text:
Thinking you missed a trick there with the lead oxide being an N type semiconductor, you could have paired it with the copper mesh (Cu2O- P-type) with the oven treatment?  Possibly not though as you'd have to make the lead coating thin enough to allow light to pass through, not sure? Semiconductors have always baffled me:-)
I absolutely love these videos Rob, I remember the early videos you referenced regarding the copper sheeting, tried that with little success and often wondered if it could be improved,  very keen to see more of your videos on this subject.

author: @MartinPHellwig
text: N and P type together should make a functional relay, which in turn you can make logic gates of which in turn can build a CPU, all by having two inkjettable slushes! Time and time again I am amazed at what you manage to figure out, very much appreciated!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: my thoughts exactly mate

replies.author: @erumaaro6060
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Hang on a bloody minute!

When i heard that the yellow lead was an n-type semiconductor, immediately thought that it could be used to make transistors using the p-type copper.
From what you two just said, it would seem that it's not just my wishfull thinking, but actually possible?
Moreover you could "paint" both materials?
Combining this whith paintable dialectric (laquer/water glass?) and conductor(graphite?) would make MOS-FETs and CMOS logic possible with very low tech tools/materials!
Although performance compared to similarly sized silicon based transistors would probly be abbismal.

Home made logic circuits from scrap materials...
That definitly has a nice ring to it! 
I think this would be an interesting idea for a video.

replies.author: @ancapftw9113
replies.text:
@erumaaro6060 there are metal oxide based transistors, so you probably could do this.  I'm hoping to do this one day, once I build a kiln.  That way I can try and make simple electronics from scratch.
Ultimate goal would be a CPU or microcontroller, but I'd need some sort of printing for that.  By hand I doubt I could do more that 1 transistor per mm, even with a ton of practice.

replies.author: @gdolphy
replies.text: @ancapftw9113 : use a desktop microscope to shrink your image. Use gimp, photoshop or an actual program for making your circuits.

replies.author: @ancapftw9113
replies.text: @gdolphy Sam Zeloof did something similar with a projector focused through a telescope.

author: @cayrex
text: lead oxide,... interesting. I never I think about the lead oxide. I try materials like ZnO, TiO2, MoO3, CuO,.... but never PbO. Good to know,... I will try this to see what I will get. I try copper oxide (p-type) and zinc oxide (n-type) which was really simple cell and I get around 0.24V. Really fast made cell I made with oxidizing the zinc and heating the copper. Or with electrodepositon of copper and zinc first and then  oxidizing,.... the voltage will not be dramatically highger but the curren will be a litle better. So, thank you for this.

replies.author: @oddjobbobb
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith hey Robert, I just want to say I really like how you encourage people to experiment and test their ideas. I found it off-putting at first because, “Hey! I want to know the answer!” But I came to realize that as any good teacher knows, a person who is given a fish if fed once, but a person who is taught to fish is fed forever, and in your YTs you give us all the knowledge we need to catch our own fish. Thank you for the high standard you hold for us, who listen and watch and learn from the time you spend producing these videos.
